verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन ।
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव ॥ १ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
arjuna uvāca
jyāyasī cet karmaṇas te matā buddhir janārdana |
tat kiṁ karmaṇi ghore māṁ niyojayasi keśava || 1 ||

anvaya.meaning: O agitator-of-people (an epithet of the Teacher)

verses.literal_meaning: The questioner said: O addressed-one, if you consider buddhi (intelligence) superior to action, why then do you engage me in this terrible action?
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.1 opens chapter 3 with the questioner's pushback. After hearing 2.49's ranking of buddhi-yoga above mere fruit-driven action, the questioner takes 'buddhi' as jñāna-niṣṭhā (knowledge-steadfastness) and concludes the call to action is contradictory. The verse's tension drives the entire chapter; per the Advaita reading, the questioner has misread sequence as contradiction; per the Vishishtadvaita reading, the question is the natural one for a seeker who has not yet integrated knowledge with action. Either way, this verse is the chapter's dialectical opening.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: the questioner's question rests on a misreading. If buddhi (= jñāna-niṣṭhā, knowledge-steadfastness) is intended as superior, and the Teacher also engages the questioner in karma, then a SEQUENCE has been misread as a CONTRADICTION. The chapter's argument is that knowledge and action are not alternatives but a sequence: karma-yoga purifies → jñāna-niṣṭhā arises.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the questioner's question is the natural one for the seeker. If firm devotion to knowledge is the means to the vision of the self, and if that devotion arises only from cessation of sensory and mental activities, then why should one engage in works that are themselves activities of the senses? The chapter is the Teacher's correction of this seeker-confusion.

positions.rendering: intelligence
knowledge
Wisdom (jñāna-niṣṭhā, steadfastness in Knowledge)
knowledge / firm devotion to knowledge

disagreements.explanation: The questioner's question hinges on what 'buddhi' means here. The Advaita reading takes it specifically as jñāna-niṣṭhā (knowledge-steadfastness) — the path of cessation of activity that culminates in self-vision. The Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as 'firm devotion to knowledge' that arises after sense-cessation. The two modern translators flatten these to 'intelligence' or 'knowledge'. The question's force depends on the strong reading: if the goal is the cessation-path, why is the Teacher telling me to engage in this terrible deed?

cross_references.explanation: The questioner's 3.1 question is the direct follow-up to 2.49, where the Teacher ranked buddhi-yoga above mere fruit-driven action. The questioner takes 'buddhi superior to action' as 'knowledge superior to action' and pushes back. The chapter is the Teacher's correction.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
व्यामिश्रेणेव वाक्येन बुद्धिं मोहयसीव मे ।
तदेकं वद निश्चित्य येन श्रेयोऽहमाप्‍नुयाम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
vyāmiśreṇeva vākyena buddhiṁ mohayasīva me |
tad ekaṁ vada niścitya yena śreyo 'ham āpnuyām || 2 ||
verses.literal_meaning: By your seemingly mixed speech, you bewilder my intellect; tell me decisively the one path by which I may attain the highest good.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.2 sharpens 3.1. The questioner names the Teacher's speech as vyāmiśra (mixed/equivocal); his buddhi is bewildered (mohayasi iva); he asks for the one path (ekam), decisively (niścitya), by which he may attain śreyas (the highest good). The verse forces the Teacher to distinguish the two paths cleanly in 3.3 rather than leave them blended. It is the seeker's plea for a single clear direction.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: the questioner takes the Teacher's earlier presentation of jñāna and karma as a confused alternation, and demands a single answer. The verse's force is the demand for clarity; the response in 3.3 will give the questioner two SEPARATE niṣṭhās rather than the combination he seems to have inferred.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: this is the legitimate plea of one who has not yet seen how the two paths integrate. Without clarity, action is paralyzed. The chapter's response gives the integration: karma-yoga is not a lesser path; it is the path that fits the questioner's situation.

positions.rendering: equivocal
ambiguous
mixed
intermingled / blended

disagreements.explanation: The questioner calls the Teacher's speech 'mixed' — but mixed in what sense? All four traditions agree the appearance is of two different teachings juxtaposed. The Advaita reading takes it as the questioner's misreading: he thinks knowledge and action are alternatives when the Teacher is teaching them as a sequence. The Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as the legitimate confusion of one who has not yet seen how the two integrate. The translation choice ('equivocal' vs 'ambiguous' vs 'mixed') reflects whether the speaker thinks the questioner is wrong (Advaita) or merely uninstructed (Vishishtadvaita).

cross_references.explanation: 3.2 sharpens 3.1's question by naming the Teacher's speech as 'mixed' and demanding one decisive answer. The pair forces the explicit two-path distinction at 3.3.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
लोकेऽस्मिन्द्विविधा निष्ठा पुरा प्रोक्ता मयानघ ।
ज्ञानयोगेन सांख्यानां कर्मयोगेन योगिनाम् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
loke 'smin dvi-vidhā niṣṭhā purā proktā mayānagha |
jñāna-yogena sāṅkhyānāṁ karma-yogena yoginām || 3 ||
verses.literal_meaning: The Lord said: O sinless one, in this world I declared previously two kinds of niṣṭhā — the jñāna-yoga of the contemplatives and the karma-yoga of the yogis.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.3 is the Teacher's first answer. He distinguishes two niṣṭhās (steadfast disciplines) declared previously: jñāna-yoga for the contemplatives (those temperamentally suited to contemplation) and karma-yoga for the yogis (those temperamentally suited to action). Per the Advaita reading the two paths are independent (rejecting the combination-theory). Per the Vishishtadvaita reading both reach the same goal but through different fitness; karma-yoga is the path being recommended TO THE QUESTIONER specifically because of his state, not because it is universally superior.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: the Teacher grants two SEPARATE niṣṭhās (against any combination-theory) — jñāna-yoga as one complete path, karma-yoga as another. The verse names karma-yoga as the questioner's path SPECIFICALLY, not because karma-yoga is universally superior, but because it is the appropriate path for someone in his state.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the two niṣṭhās suit different states of inner purity. jñāna-yoga of the contemplatives suits those whose old karma has been purified and whose inner instrument is already subdued; karma-yoga of the yogis suits those whose purification proceeds through action. Both reach the same goal, but through different routes appropriate to fitness.

positions.rendering: two classes of men
two paths leading to enlightenment
two-fold steadfastness / niṣṭhā as standing-firm in a discipline
two-fold disciplines / fitness for two paths

disagreements.explanation: niṣṭhā literally means 'standing firm, steadfastness' — the locked-in commitment to a particular discipline. The Advaita reading emphasizes that the verse declares two SEPARATE niṣṭhās (against any combination-theory): jñāna-yoga as one complete path, karma-yoga as another. The Vishishtadvaita reading emphasizes that the two niṣṭhās suit different temperaments / states of inner purity, but both reach the same goal. Prabhupada's 'two classes of men' captures the temperamental sorting; Mukundananda's 'two paths' captures the path-distinction. None is wrong; the disagreement is about whether the two paths are independent (Advaita) or sequenced (Vishishtadvaita: karma-yoga purifies → jñāna-yoga arises).

cross_references.explanation: 3.3 introduces the two paths; 3.4 explains why the renunciate path of mere abstention does NOT work, sealing the case for karma-yoga as the path the questioner must walk.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्न‍ुते ।
न च सन्न्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
na karmaṇām anārambhān naiṣkarmyaṁ puruṣo 'śnute |
na ca sannyasanād eva siddhiṁ samadhigacchati || 4 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Not by non-undertaking of action does a person attain naiṣkarmya (freedom from action), nor by mere renunciation does one attain perfection.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.4 begins the famous dual claim. naiṣkarmya — the state of being free from action — is NOT attained by non-undertaking (anārambhāt); siddhi (perfection) is NOT attained by mere renunciation (sannyasanād eva). Both ācāryas read this as the foundational rejection of the questioner's strategy of laying down arms. Per the Advaita reading: the karma-yogin purifies the mind through action and only then becomes eligible for jñāna-niṣṭhā; abstaining is not a shortcut. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: success comes from acts done without attachment, performed as worship of the Supreme Person; without these, jñāna-niṣṭhā cannot arise. Doctrinally pivotal: the renunciate path is real, but you do not enter it by quitting.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading is sharp: naiṣkarmya = the state of abiding in one's own Self which is free from action; this state is NOT attained by anārambhāt — by mere non-undertaking — of sacrifices and prescribed action. Prescribed action purifies the mind by attenuating sins; without that purification, jñāna-niṣṭhā cannot arise. Renunciation alone (sannyasanād eva) without prior purification yields nothing. The verse rejects the apparently quickest route — just stop — as actually the slowest, because it skips the prerequisite.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: 'Not by non-performance of the acts prescribed by the scriptures does a person attain freedom from Karma (i.e., Jñāna Yoga); nor by ceasing to perform such actions...' Success comes through actions done WITHOUT ATTACHMENT TO THE FRUITS, performed AS WORSHIP OF THE SUPREME PERSON. Without that orientation, jñāna-niṣṭhā cannot arise. The verse rules out abstention as a strategy and replaces it with karma-yoga-as-worship.

positions.rendering: freedom from reaction / renunciation alone
freedom from karmic reactions / mere physical renunciation
naiṣkarmya = state of abiding in one's Self which is free from action; not attained by non-performance of sacrifices and other prescribed actions
naiṣkarmya = jñāna-yoga; not attained by non-performance of scriptural acts; success comes through actions done without attachment to fruits, by way of worshipping the Supreme Person

disagreements.explanation: naiṣkarmya is the state of being free from action's binding. Both ācāryas agree: the state cannot be reached by abstention. The Advaita reading takes naiṣkarmya as Self-abidance, with karma-yoga as the purificatory pre-stage. The Vishishtadvaita reading takes naiṣkarmya as jñāna-niṣṭhā arising from karma-yoga performed as worship. The doctrinal disagreement is about WHAT karma-yoga's role is (preparation vs. own-mode of worship), but both reject the strategy of 'don't act, become free.' Modern translators preserve the rejection but flatten the underlying disagreement.

cross_references.explanation: 2.47 already warned 'mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi' — let no attachment to inaction be yours. 3.4 develops the metaphysical underpinning: non-action is not even possible, so attachment to it is doubly mistaken.
3.5 supplies the mechanism for 3.4: prakṛti's guṇas drive action helplessly; so non-undertaking cannot achieve naiṣkarmya.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत् ।
कार्यते ह्यवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
na hi kaścit kṣaṇam api jātu tiṣṭhaty akarma-kṛt |
kāryate hy avaśaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛti-jair guṇaiḥ || 5 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Indeed, no one ever stays even for a moment without performing action; everyone is helplessly compelled to act by the guṇas born of prakṛti.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.5 is the metaphysical claim under 3.4. na hi kaścit kṣaṇam api jātu tiṣṭhaty akarma-kṛt — no one EVER stays even a moment without performing action. Why? kāryate hy avaśaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛti-jair guṇaiḥ — everyone is HELPLESSLY (avaśaḥ) made to act by the guṇas born of prakṛti. Per the Advaita reading: this applies to the unenlightened; the man of realization is treated separately. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: until the old accumulation of karma is annulled, the guṇas drive the actor. The verse closes off the fantasy of action-cessation. Action is not a choice that can be declined; the question is only the kind of action and the orientation of the actor.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading restricts the verse's scope: 'the word unenlightened has to be added to the sentence, since the men of realization have been spoken of separately.' The unenlightened are made to act helplessly (avaśaḥ) by the guṇas; the man of jñāna-niṣṭhā is treated apart. The verse's force, on this reading: for the actor still in saṁsāra, action-cessation is not possible; therefore karma-yoga (action with purification of mind) is the only available path until jñāna-niṣṭhā is attained.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the verse is a structural claim about Prakṛti's compulsion: the guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) increase in accordance with one's old karma; the actor is helplessly driven. jñāna-yoga can be attained only after the inner organ is purified by annulling old accumulation through karma-yoga. The verse's force: karma-yoga is the means by which the very compulsion the verse names is broken.

positions.rendering: forced to act helplessly / according to the qualities he has acquired
compelled to act by their qualities born of material nature
avaśaḥ = under compulsion; the unenlightened are made to work by the gunas; the verse applies to the unenlightened (men of realization treated separately)
compelled to act according to the gunas born of Prakrti, which increase in accordance with one's old karma; jnana yoga only attainable after annulling old accumulation through karma yoga

disagreements.explanation: The disagreement is about the SCOPE of the claim. The Advaita reading explicitly limits 3.5 to the unenlightened: the man of realization (sthita-prajña, jñāna-niṣṭha) is treated separately and is NOT compelled by the guṇas in the same sense. The Vishishtadvaita reading extends the claim further into Prakṛti's compulsion via accumulated karma; karma-yoga is the means to break the compulsion. Modern translators leave the scope unstated, which can read as a universal claim about all beings forever — sharper than either ācārya intended.

cross_references.explanation: 2.47's 'mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi' is given its strongest defense here: not just 'don't be attached to inaction' but 'inaction is not even possible'.
18.59 echoes this: 'if you think you will not fight, prakṛti will engage you'. The same metaphysical claim recurs at the chapter-18 close.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य य आस्ते मनसा स्मरन् ।
इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः स उच्यते ॥ ६ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya ya āste manasā smaran |
indriyārthān vimūḍhātmā mithyācāraḥ sa ucyate || 6 ||
verses.literal_meaning: One who restrains the action-organs while sitting and mentally remembering the objects of the senses — that one of deluded self is called a hypocrite (mithyācāra).
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.6 names a sharp pathology. The one who restrains the action-organs (karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya) while sitting and mentally remembering (manasā smaran) the objects of the senses — that vimūḍha-ātmā (deluded self) is mithyācāra, hypocrite. Per the Advaita reading: the gloss is direct — sah ucyate mithyācāraḥ, sinful person of false conduct. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: this is the seeker whose inner organ is not subdued, whose mind drifts to objects while his external posture mimics restraint; his ācāra (conduct) belies his profession. The verse names a recognizable failure mode: surface withdrawal coupled with interior agitation.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading is direct: 'yaḥ saṁyamya karma-indriyāṇi āste manasā smaran indriya-arthān, sah vimūḍha-ātmā, ucyate mithyā-ācāraḥ — a hypocrite, a sinful person.' The verse names the failure mode and judges it severely. The hypocrite is not merely confused; he is committing a real spiritual offense — his outward conduct contradicts his inner state.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita diagnosis: the seeker whose inner and outer organs are not yet conquered (sins not annulled), but who nonetheless struggles for self-knowledge — his mind is forced to turn toward sense-objects; his actions belie his professions. He becomes 'perverted' by attempting jñāna-niṣṭhā prematurely. The verse names the consequence of skipping karma-yoga: the would-be jñāna-yogin without purification is a hypocrite, not because he is dishonest, but because his structure is misaligned.

positions.rendering: pretender
hypocrites
hypocrite, a sinful person
hypocrite, because his actions are at variance with his professions; he becomes perverted

disagreements.explanation: All four traditions render mithyācāra hard. mithyā = false; ācāra = conduct. Both ācāryas use 'hypocrite' explicitly. The Advaita reading additionally calls him 'sinful person' (saintly = the inverse); the Vishishtadvaita reading adds the structural diagnosis: profession ≠ action, leading to spiritual perversion. Prabhupada's 'pretender' is somewhat softer than Mukundananda's 'hypocrites' or the ācāryas' direct rendering. Engineering layers must preserve the directness — the verse is not consoling but diagnostic.

cross_references.explanation: 2.61 named the steady-mind ideal: senses controlled, thoughts not running on objects. 3.6 names the failure mode: senses controlled OUTSIDE while objects are mentally rehearsed INSIDE. The hypocrite is the inverse of the sthita-prajña.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
यस्त्विन्द्रियाणि मनसा नियम्यारभतेऽर्जुन ।
कर्मेन्द्रियैः कर्मयोगमसक्तः स विशिष्यते ॥ ७ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yas tv indriyāṇi manasā niyamyārabhate 'rjuna |
karmendriyaiḥ karma-yogam asaktaḥ sa viśiṣyate || 7 ||
verses.literal_meaning: But, O addressed-one, one who controls the senses with the mind and, unattached, undertakes karma-yoga with the action-organs is the superior one.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.7 inverts 3.6. yas tu — but the one who, having controlled the senses with the mind (manasā niyamya), engages the action-organs in karma-yoga (karmendriyaiḥ karma-yogam) without attachment — that one is viśiṣyate (the distinguished, the superior). Per the Advaita reading the path here is not superficial sense-restraint but real attention-discipline that issues into engaged unattached action. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading the seeker who has subdued the inner instrument can perform karma-yoga genuinely; this superiority is over the hypocrite of 3.6. The verse pairs with 3.6: do not be the false renunciate; be the karma-yogin whose senses are actually controlled.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: 3.7 is superior to the path of 3.6 (defective renunciation). The karma-yogin who has actually controlled the senses with the mind, then engages the action-organs in karma-yoga without attachment — this is the genuine path, free from the hypocrite's defects. The pairing 3.6/3.7 makes karma-yoga's superiority over false-renunciation explicit.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: 3.7 is the karma-yogin who has actually subdued the inner organ: senses controlled by the mind, action-organs engaged in karma-yoga, all without attachment. This person is viśiṣyate — distinguished, superior — over the hypocrite of 3.6. The path is not external withdrawal but real interior discipline issuing into engaged action.

positions.rendering: by far superior
certainly superior
this karma-yoga, free from such defects, is superior
this karma-yogin is distinguished / superior to the hypocrite of 3.6

disagreements.explanation: All four agree viśiṣyate = is the distinguished one, is superior. The disagreement is about the SCOPE of the comparison. The Advaita reading takes it as karma-yoga vs. defective renunciation (the path of 3.6); the Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as the karma-yogin vs. the hypocrite specifically. Both readings preserve the structural pairing of 3.6 and 3.7 — false withdrawal contrasted with genuine attention-discipline that issues into action.

cross_references.explanation: 2.61's 'manaḥ saṁyamya' (controlling the mind) is the same operation 3.7 names — but here the mind-control issues into engaged karma-yoga, not into withdrawal. The two verses form one teaching: real attention-discipline first, then action.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
नियतं कुरु कर्म त्वं कर्म ज्यायो ह्यकर्मणः ।
शरीरयात्रापि च ते न प्रसिद्ध्येदकर्मणः ॥ ८ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
niyataṁ kuru karma tvaṁ karma jyāyo hy akarmaṇaḥ |
śarīra-yātrāpi ca te na prasidhyed akarmaṇaḥ || 8 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Perform your prescribed (niyata) action — action is indeed better than inaction; even your bodily maintenance would not be possible without action.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.8 is the crisp directive. niyataṁ kuru karma tvam — perform your prescribed (regulated) action; karma jyāyo hy akarmaṇaḥ — action is better than inaction. The pragmatic floor: śarīra-yātrāpi ca te na prasidhyed akarmaṇaḥ — even your bodily maintenance would not succeed without action. Per the Advaita reading: niyata-karma = nityam, regulated daily duty; the verse argues from necessity to value. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: prescribed action covers obligatory and occasional acts; their performance, freed from attachment, becomes the means. The verse is the chapter's most direct doctrinal statement: act, because action is necessary AND superior to inaction.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: niyata = nityam (regulated daily duty); the verse argues from necessity (without action, even bodily maintenance fails) to the value-claim (action is better than inaction). On this reading, this is the chapter's pragmatic floor: until the actor reaches jñāna-niṣṭhā, action is required AND superior to abstention. The reasoning is consequentialist (necessity) backed by metaphysics (action's purificatory function).
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: niyata-karma covers obligatory (nitya) and occasional (naimittika) acts taught in the Veda. Their performance, freed from attachment, becomes the means of purification. The verse's pragmatic claim — even bodily maintenance fails without action — backs the metaphysical claim — action is better than inaction. The whole frame: do the prescribed action as worship; do not abstain.

positions.rendering: prescribed duty
prescribed Vedic duties
niyata = nityam (regulated, daily); obligatory action
obligatory and occasional acts taught in the Veda

disagreements.explanation: niyata literally = regulated, fixed, prescribed. The Advaita reading takes it as nitya-karma (daily obligatory action — the constant duty). The Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as covering both nitya (obligatory) and naimittika (occasional) acts. Modern translators say 'prescribed duty' which is general enough to be uncontroversial. The substantive point both ācāryas preserve: action is not arbitrary; what is endorsed is the duty that has already been assigned by one's role and station.

cross_references.explanation: 2.47's 'karmaṇy evādhikāras te' becomes 3.8's 'niyataṁ kuru karma tvam' — the abstract injunction made concrete: do the action that has been assigned to you.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं कर्मबन्धनः ।
तदर्थं कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर ॥ ९ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yajñārthāt karmaṇo 'nyatra loko 'yaṁ karma-bandhanaḥ |
tad-arthaṁ karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samācara || 9 ||
verses.literal_meaning: This world is bound by action other than action performed for yajña (sacrifice); therefore, son of Kuntī, perform action for that purpose, free from attachment.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.9 is the yajña pivot. yajñārthāt karmaṇo 'nyatra loko 'yaṁ karma-bandhanaḥ — this world (or this person) is bound by action other than that performed for the sake of yajña. tad-arthaṁ karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samācara — therefore perform action for that purpose, free from attachment. Per the Advaita reading: yajña = Viṣṇu (per the Vedic 'sacrifice is verily Viṣṇu'); whatever is done for him is yajña-artham. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: yajña includes the prescribed sacrifices and the broader complex of duty-performed-as-worship; the world is imprisoned by personal-end action and freed by yajña-oriented action. The verse establishes the FRAME that distinguishes binding from non-binding action.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: yajña = Viṣṇu (citing Tai. Sam. 1.7.4: 'Sacrifice is verily Viṣṇu'). Action FOR yajña = action for God. Action other than this binds the world (loko 'yam karma-bandhanaḥ); action for yajña, performed mukta-saṅgaḥ (free from attachment), does not bind. The verse establishes the FRAME: orientation toward yajña (= toward God) is what distinguishes binding from non-binding action. The action itself is identical; the orientation differs.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: yajñārthāt karmaṇaḥ broadly: action for the purpose of sacrifice prescribed in the scriptures, AND any acquisition or work performed in the spirit of yajña. The world is imprisoned by the bond of work only when work is done for personal ends; for sacrifice, no bondage. mukta-saṅgaḥ samācara — perform the work, having overcome attachments generated by personal ambition. The verse establishes the frame; everything in 3.10-3.16 elaborates the cosmology that supports the frame.

positions.rendering: as a sacrifice for Viṣṇu
as a yajna to the Supreme Lord
for the sake of yajña, where yajña = Viṣṇu (per Tai. Sam. 1.7.4: 'Sacrifice is verily Viṣṇu')
for the purpose of sacrifice prescribed in the scriptures; broader: any acquisition or work done in the spirit of yajña

disagreements.explanation: yajñārthāt is the verse's frame-pivot. The Advaita reading takes yajña = Viṣṇu (the deity behind sacrifice); the action is for God. The Vishishtadvaita reading takes yajña as both the literal sacrifices and the broader scope of any work performed as worship. Prabhupada inherits the Advaita gloss explicitly. Mukundananda preserves the theistic frame. The doctrinally important point all four agree on: the FRAME of action's purpose distinguishes binding from non-binding action. Engineering layers must preserve this — the action itself is not the pivot; the orientation toward yajña is.

cross_references.explanation: 2.39 introduced karma-yoga as a discipline. 2.50 named yoga as kauśalam in action. 3.9 supplies the missing piece: the FRAME — action for yajña — is what makes karma-yoga non-binding.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा पुरोवाच प्रजापतिः ।
अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वमेष वोऽस्त्विष्टकामधुक् ॥ १० ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
saha-yajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛṣṭvā purovāca prajāpatiḥ |
anena prasaviṣyadhvam eṣa vo 'stv iṣṭa-kāma-dhuk || 10 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Having created creatures along with yajña, the Lord of creatures of old said: 'By this you shall multiply; let it be your wish-fulfilling cow.'
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.10 begins the cosmic argument. The Lord of creatures, having created beings along with yajña (saha-yajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛṣṭvā), said in the beginning: 'By this you shall multiply / prosper (prasaviṣyadhvam); let this be your iṣṭa-kāma-dhuk (wish-fulfilling cow / source of desired ends).' Per the Advaita reading: the wheel was set in motion by Prajāpati at creation; yajña is not later add-on but constitutive of the cosmos. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: Prajāpati instructs the creatures that yajña is the path of mutual increase. The verse names yajña as cosmologically primordial, not metaphor.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: 3.10 cosmologically: Prajāpati at the beginning of creation set in motion the wheel by creating creatures along with yajña. The wheel is not a later development but constitutive of the cosmos. The verse establishes yajña as primordial obligation; the engineering analog must register that the verse claims metaphysical-cosmological scope, not merely operational metaphor.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: Prajāpati is the Supreme Person's creative agent. The instruction at creation — 'by this you shall multiply / prosper; let it be your wish-fulfilling cow' — establishes yajña as the path of mutual increase. The devas are real persons; the Supreme Person abides as their inner Self. The verse's metaphysics is robust; the engineering analog is at most a pale operational shadow.

positions.rendering: sent forth generations of men and demigods, along with sacrifices for Viṣṇu
Brahma created humankind along with duties (yajñas)
Prajāpati created creatures together with yajña; yajña was set in motion at creation
Prajāpati created beings along with yajña and instructed them in mutual increase through yajña

disagreements.explanation: All four agree the wheel was created by Prajāpati at creation. The disagreement: who is Prajāpati? Prabhupada (Gaudiya): Prajāpati is the creator-deity; sacrifice is for Viṣṇu specifically. Mukundananda: Brahma is the creator. The Advaita reading: Prajāpati as cosmological agent. The Vishishtadvaita reading: Prajāpati as the Supreme Person's creative agent. The engineering analog must register honestly that the verse claims yajña is COSMOLOGICALLY PRIMORDIAL — not later metaphor — and that the engineering layer's 'sustaining loop' is an analog of operational scope, not of cosmic origination.

cross_references.explanation: 3.9 named yajña as the frame; 3.10-3.15 supplies the cosmological grounding.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
देवान्भावयतानेन ते देवा भावयन्तु वः ।
परस्परं भावयन्तः श्रेयः परमवाप्स्यथ ॥ ११ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
devān bhāvayatānena te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ |
parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ śreyaḥ param avāpsyatha || 11 ||
verses.literal_meaning: By this (yajña) nourish the gods; let those gods nourish you; nourishing each other, you shall attain the supreme good.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.11 names the mutual nourishment. devān bhāvayatānena — by this (yajña) nourish the gods; te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ — let the gods nourish you. parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ śreyaḥ param avāpsyatha — nourishing each other, you shall attain the supreme good. Per the Advaita reading: the gods are pleased by oblations and reciprocate with rain, food, and worldly goods. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: the devas are real persons; the Supreme Person abides as their inner Self; the mutual nourishment is real cosmic exchange. The verse states: the wheel is mutual; withdrawal from one side breaks the other.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: the mutual nourishment is cosmological-natural: oblations to the gods reach the celestial agents (Indra et al.) responsible for natural processes (especially rain), and they reciprocate by sustaining the world. The wheel is mutual; withdrawal from one side breaks the other. The verse uses parasparam (mutually) to insist on the bidirectionality.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the devas are real persons with the Supreme Person as their inner Self. The mutual nourishment is real cosmic exchange, not metaphor. parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ śreyaḥ param avāpsyatha — by mutual nourishment you attain the supreme good. Engineering layers must NOT collapse devas into 'team abstractions' — the Vishishtadvaita reading specifically rules this out.

positions.rendering: demigods, being pleased by sacrifices, will also please you
celestial gods will be pleased; cooperation between humans and the celestial gods
the gods are pleased by oblations and reciprocate with rain, food, and worldly goods
the devas are real persons with the Supreme Person as their inner Self; mutual nourishment is real cosmic exchange

disagreements.explanation: The four readings agree on the mechanism (mutual nourishment) but differ on the ontology of the devas. Prabhupada and Mukundananda use 'demigods/celestial gods' with personal reference; the Advaita reading takes them as cosmological agents responsible for natural processes (rain especially); the Vishishtadvaita reading insists they are real persons with the Supreme Person as their inner Self. Engineering layers should NOT collapse devas into 'team abstractions' — the Vishishtadvaita reading specifically rules this out. The analog must be acknowledged as analog.

cross_references.explanation: 3.10 stated yajña was created with creatures; 3.11 names the mutual nourishment between gods and creatures that the wheel constitutes.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः ।
तैर्दत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः ॥ १२ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
iṣṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ |
tair dattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ || 12 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Indeed, the gods, nourished by yajña, will give you desired enjoyments; one who enjoys what is given by them without offering back is verily a thief.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.12 sharpens the claim with the thief diagnosis. iṣṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ — the gods, nourished by yajña, will give you desired enjoyments. tair dattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ — but one who enjoys what is given by them without offering back is verily a thief (stena). Per the Advaita reading: the verse fixes the obligation as cosmological-ethical; consumption without contribution = theft. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: the devas, with the Supreme Person as their inner Self, supply the means of life; not returning offering is misappropriation. The verse uses sharp language: stena, thief.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: the gods give iṣṭān bhogān (desired enjoyments) when nourished by yajña; the one who consumes their gifts without offering back is verily a thief (stena eva saḥ). The cosmological-ethical structure makes consumption-without-contribution into theft. This is not metaphor; it is a structural claim about the wheel.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the devas, with the Supreme Person as their inner Self, supply the means of life (rain, sun, food, breath); offering back (yajña) is the proper return; not returning is misappropriation of what is His. stena eva saḥ — verily a thief. The verse's ethical-metaphysical structure makes consumption-without-offering into theft against the Supreme Person.

positions.rendering: certainly a thief
verily thieves
thief; consumption without contribution = misappropriation
thief; the devas, with the Supreme Person as their inner Self, supply the means of life — not returning offering is misappropriation of what is His

disagreements.explanation: All four agree stena is the right rendering: thief. The verse uses sharp ethical-cosmological language. The disagreement is only in the metaphysical anchor of the obligation: the Advaita reading takes it as cosmological-ethical (the wheel's structure makes consumption-without-contribution theft); the Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as theft against the Supreme Person specifically (the devas are His agents). Engineering layers must preserve the sharpness — stena is not a soft word — without overclaiming the metaphysical anchor.

cross_references.explanation: 3.11 named the mutual nourishment; 3.12 sharpens with the thief diagnosis — consumption without offering is theft.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तो मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः ।
भुञ्जते ते त्वघं पापा ये पचन्त्यात्मकारणात् ॥ १३ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yajña-śiṣṭāśinaḥ santo mucyante sarva-kilbiṣaiḥ |
bhuñjate te tv aghaṁ pāpā ye pacanty ātma-kāraṇāt || 13 ||
verses.literal_meaning: The good, eaters of the remnants of yajña, are released from all sins; but the wicked, who cook for their own sake, eat sin.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.13 is sharper still. yajña-śiṣṭāśinaḥ santaḥ mucyante sarva-kilbiṣaiḥ — the saintly, eaters of yajña remnants, are released from ALL sins. bhuñjate te tv aghaṁ pāpā ye pacanty ātma-kāraṇāt — but those wicked who cook for their OWN sake EAT SIN (agham). Per the Advaita reading: the householder's pañca-mahā-yajña (five great daily offerings) feeds gods, manes, humans, beings, sages; what remains becomes nectar, the food the householder eats. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: the offering is to the Supreme Person abiding as the Self of all deities. The verse uses agham bhuñjate — they eat sin — as a literal claim, not metaphor. Engineering layers MUST preserve the sharpness.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: the verse is grounded in the householder's pañca-mahā-yajña (five great daily offerings — to gods, manes, humans, beings, sages). The remnants (yajña-śiṣṭa) become the food the householder eats; this food is nectar, freeing from sin. The wicked who cook for themselves alone (ātma-kāraṇāt), without offering, eat sin (agham bhuñjate). 'Eat sin' is taken literally — the act has a real karmic structure.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: 'Those who acquire food materials solely for propitiating the Supreme Person abiding as the Self of Indra and other deities, and who, after cooking them, propitiate the Supreme Person as He is, and then sustain themselves on the remnants — they alone will be free of impurities... But evil-minded are those who acquire for selfish use what was meant for offering — they eat sin.' The metaphysical anchor is the Supreme Person as the Self of all deities; the offering reaches Him through them.

positions.rendering: verily eat only sin
verily eat only sin
those who cook for themselves alone (without offering) are eaters of sin; the householder's five great offerings are required
the evil-minded who acquire for selfish use without offering eat sin; right consumption is yajña-remnant after offering to the Supreme Person abiding as the Self of all

disagreements.explanation: All four traditions render this verse extremely sharp. agham = sin, evil; bhuñjate = they eat. The verse is NOT metaphor — both ācāryas treat 'eat sin' as a literal claim about the karmic structure of unoffered consumption. The Advaita reading grounds it in the householder's pañca-mahā-yajña obligation; the Vishishtadvaita reading grounds it in the relation of all consumption to the Supreme Person. The disagreement is only in the cosmological anchor. Engineering layers MUST preserve the sharpness; softening to 'inefficient' or 'imbalanced' consumption misreads the verse.

cross_references.explanation: 3.12 named the thief; 3.13 names the eater-of-sin. Two registers of the same diagnosis.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
अन्नाद्भ‍वन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः ।
यज्ञा‍द्भ‍‍वति पर्जन्यो यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भ‍वः ॥ १४ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
annād bhavanti bhūtāni parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ |
yajñād bhavati parjanyo yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ || 14 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Beings come into being from food; food comes from rain; rain comes from yajña; yajña is born of action.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.14 begins the cosmic chain. annād bhavanti bhūtāni — beings come from food; parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ — food's origin is rain; yajñād bhavati parjanyaḥ — rain comes from yajña; yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ — yajña is born of action. Per the Advaita reading (citing Manu 6.76): oblations poured into fire reach the sun, from the sun comes rain, from rain comes food. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: the chain is metaphysically real; yajña and the prescribed actions that produce it are required for the chain to remain intact. The verse names the loop: action → yajña → rain → food → beings → action.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading grounds the chain in Vedic cosmology, citing Manu 6.76: oblations properly poured into fire reach the sun; from the sun comes rain; from rain comes food; from food, beings. The chain is metaphysically real, not metaphor. yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ — yajña arises from karma (the action of performing the rite). The verse's cosmological reach is large; engineering analogs must register honestly that the analog is a pale shadow of the original cosmic claim.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading: the chain at the operational layer: from food arise beings; from rain food is produced (matters of common experience). From sacrifice comes rain (per Manu 6.76). yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ — sacrifice is born of action (the agent's act of collecting materials, etc.). karma in turn arises from brahman (the body / Prakṛti). The chain is metaphysically real but the operational reading is kept visible.

positions.rendering: yajña is born of prescribed duties
sacrifice is produced by the performance of prescribed duties
yajña arises from karma (action); citing Manu 6.76 — oblations into fire reach the sun, from sun comes rain, from rain comes food
yajña is born of the activities of collecting materials etc. by the agent; activity arises from Brahman / the body born of Prakṛti

disagreements.explanation: All four traditions preserve the chain: anna → bhūta → vṛṣṭi → yajña → karma. The verse states a metaphysical-cosmological loop. The Advaita reading cites Manu directly to show the chain is real (oblations literally produce rain). The Vishishtadvaita reading takes karma here as the activity of collecting materials and performing the rite, with brahma in 3.15 then naming the Prakṛti from which activity arises. Engineering analogs to this verse are inherently STRETCHED: the original is cosmological, not metaphor; the engineering layer can register a sustaining loop honestly only at the operational layer.

cross_references.explanation: 3.14's chain (anna → bhūta → parjanya → yajña → karma) is the cosmological mechanism that 3.9 invoked when it said 'action other than yajña binds'.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भ‍वं विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भ‍वम् ।
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् ॥ १५ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
karma brahmodbhavaṁ viddhi brahmākṣara-samudbhavam |
tasmāt sarva-gataṁ brahma nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam || 15 ||
verses.literal_meaning: Know that action is born of Brahman, and Brahman is born of the Imperishable; therefore the all-pervading Brahman is eternally established in yajña.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.15 closes the cosmic chain into Brahman. karma brahmodbhavaṁ viddhi — know that action is born of Brahman; brahmākṣara-samudbhavam — Brahman is born of the Imperishable (akṣara); tasmāt sarva-gataṁ brahma nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam — therefore the all-pervading Brahman is eternally established in yajña. Per the Advaita reading: brahma here can mean the Veda (its source is the Imperishable); action arises from the Veda's prescription. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: brahma here = the body / the Prakṛti from which action arises; yajña is rooted in the cosmic Person. Both readings preserve: yajña is metaphysically anchored, not optional. The verse's cosmological reach is large.
verses.shankara_summary: The Advaita reading: brahma in this verse = the Veda. karma is born of brahman (the Vedic prescription); brahman is born of the Imperishable (akṣara, the eternal source). Therefore the all-pervading Veda-Brahman is eternally established in yajña. The verse closes the chain into the Veda's authority and into the akṣara's metaphysical primacy. The engineering layer cannot honestly carry this; it must be tagged STRETCHED.
verses.ramanuja_summary: The Vishishtadvaita reading of brahma here is distinctive: the term refers to the physical body, the modifications of Prakṛti, from which action arises. The reading cites Mun. U. 1.1.9 and Gita 14.3 ('this great Brahman is my womb'). Therefore action is produced by the physical body, which is itself rooted in Prakṛti, which is rooted in the Supreme Person. The verse closes the chain into divine cosmology. The disagreement with the Advaita reading (Veda-as-source vs. Prakṛti-as-source) is doctrinally significant but does not change the engineering-relevant directive: yajña is metaphysically anchored, not optional.

positions.rendering: the Vedas (regulated activities are prescribed in the Vedas, and the Vedas are directly manifested from the Supreme Personality of Godhead)
the Vedas / God Himself
brahma = the Veda; the Veda is born of the Imperishable; therefore the all-pervading Veda-Brahman is established in yajña
brahma here = the physical body / Prakṛti from which action arises; later (14.3) the Lord says 'this great Brahman is my womb'

disagreements.explanation: 3.15's brahma is the chapter's most contested reading. The Advaita reading takes it as the Veda (the textual source from which prescribed action is known), born of the akṣara (Imperishable). The Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as Prakṛti / the body, the source from which action arises. Both preserve: yajña is metaphysically anchored; karma is not free-floating. The disagreement is doctrinally significant (Veda-as-source vs. Prakṛti-as-source) but does not change the verse's directive force. Engineering layers MUST tag this verse STRETCHED — both readings reach metaphysics that the engineering layer cannot honestly carry.

cross_references.explanation: 3.14's chain ends in karma; 3.15 takes karma further back into brahman and akṣara, completing the cosmological grounding before chapter 3 turns at 3.16 to the 'wheel-breaker' charge.
